responseSet.authorCitation: Ramires, V. R., Schwan, S. & Midgley, N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mentalization-based therapy with maltreated children living in shelters in southern Brazil: A single case study

Vera Ramires , Soraia Schwan and Nick Midgley

Nick Midgley

UNISINOS, Brazil

London, UK

Nick Midgley  

children's psychotherapy, mentalization, depression in children, shelter, qualitative research, case studies

This study describes the mentalization-based child therapy of a boy who suffered from early abuse and neglect, who was living in a shelter in southern Brazil. This single case study aimed at assessing whether this kind of psychotherapy contributes to reducing depressive symptoms and developing a greater capacity for reflection on the mental states of self and other, as part of developing a more coherent sense of self. Structured assessments were conducted before therapy and after six months of treatment, and the audio-recorded treatment sessions were analyzed using content analysis, in order to identify key themes in the treatment itself. The results suggested a significant improvement in depressive symptoms and some changes in mentalization with the beginnings of a movement toward a more cohesive and integrated self. We argue that mentalization-based child therapy could be a promising therapeutic approach for children who have experienced severe disruptions of emotional bonds, due to the way it focuses on the capacity to regulate affect and develop a more coherent sense of self.

responseSet.title: Front-line services, complexity, research and policy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Front-line services, complexity, research and policy

Andrew Cooper and Bernadette Wren

Bernadette Wren

London, UK

Bernadette Wren  

complexity, causality, objectivity, mental health research, social policy

The evidence-based culture of modern mental health policy and service delivery is being extended through funded efforts to accelerate implementation of research findings in practice settings. In this paper, we argue that this linear model of research and policy-making is profoundly ill adapted to the nature of practice realities and real-world policy-making processes. Both need to be re-conceptualised using the theoretical and practical resources deriving from modern complexity theory. A short case study illustrates the nature of ‘complexity’, the reframing of the notion of ‘evidence’ it implies and the different relationship between research, practice and policy that flows from this more attuned model of mental health and therapeutic processes.

responseSet.title: Responding to self-harm in children and adolescents. A professional's guide to identification, intervention and support
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Responding to self-harm in children and adolescents. A professional's guide to identification, intervention and support

William Crouch

William Crouch

London, UK

William Crouch  

Steven Walker, Jessica Kingsley Publishers, 2012, 144 pp., (paperback)

Responding to and working with young people and children who self-harm is challenging and anxiety provoking, often causing us to feel deskilled. This then is an important area and I began reading this book with interest. It has easy to navigate and has logically sequenced chapters on understanding self-harm, recognition and treatment, early intervention and prevention and supporting a self-harming child. There are interesting appendices on government strategy and on the management of self-harm in primary and secondary care. There is also a list of useful resources and organisations.

The author makes explicit that the book is aimed at nurses, social workers, youth workers and teachers and would be a good reference for any primary mental health worker. I am sure it would be very useful to this group. He aspires to provide a foundation of theoretical ideas and practical guidance for a professional audience and the book is indeed brief, readable and user friendly. However, I did not think it would be particularly useful for psychotherapists other than those who are very inexperienced or who feel out of their depth with this group of patients or service users.

In terms of content, there were some really useful sections and some that I found lacking in evidence and depth. The section on understanding self-harm includes some discussion of what self-harm is and who is likely to engage in it. It also highlights the risk to socially excluded groups. There is then a rather too brief section on models: biological, psychological, environmental factors, etc. which unfortunately does not discuss any of the evidence for the models. Following this there was what I thought was a useful highlighting of some of the myths about self-harm: ‘it's for attention’, ‘it doesn't hurt’ and so on.

In the next chapter, ‘Recognising and treating self-harm’, there is acknowledgement of the minimal training that most professionals have in working with the issue: this is of course a matter that should concern us all. There is here a section on models which discusses the advantages and disadvantages of various approaches, which might be useful to a beginner in psychological treatment or those with little formal training. The following section on risk assessment I thought was very useful as a checklist of factors one would want to hold in mind.

responseSet.title: On the nature and standing of psychoanalytic psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the nature and standing of psychoanalytic psychotherapy

R. Hobson

R. Hobson

London, UK

R. Hobson  

psychoanalytic psychotherapy, scientific method, transference, technique, COWDUNG

In this paper, I offer some reflections on the standing of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in the National Health Service (NHS), with special reference to contemporary forms of evidence-based thinking. I consider what may follow when a narrow view of science or psychotherapeutic practice becomes conventional wisdom and assumes dominance. I place stress on the potential loss to NHS psychology and psychiatry if psychoanalytic psychotherapy becomes increasingly marginalized on the one hand, or overly standardized on the other.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley


London, UK

Jessica Yakeley  

It is a tremendous privilege to edit a journal and so it is with much excitement, albeit accompanied by some trepidation, that I am taking over the Editorship of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy from my predecessor in this role Alessandra Lemma. Alessandra is an extraordinarily gifted writer, researcher and clinician, and these qualities have been very evident in her inspirational leadership of the journal over the past three years. She has not only successfully steered the journal through very difficult times, but during her tenure she has also enabled the journal to grow both quantitatively and qualitatively, evidenced by an increase in the number of excellent papers submitted to the journal and a corresponding expansion of its readership.

This is remarkable given the ongoing challenges, and even hostility from certain quarters, presented by the external environment towards psychoanalytic thinking. These challenges are particularly acute in the UK where public sector services are currently facing unprecedented cuts in funding coinciding with a major reorganization of the ways in which services are commissioned to produce a climate in which psychoanalytic psychotherapy services have been arguably disadvantaged more than others. I am therefore very grateful to Alessandra not only for ensuring the survival of the journal but also for handing over one that is thriving through the scope and excellence of its papers. It seems to me more pressing than ever that we should continue to build on the work of Alessandra and her colleagues to make certain that Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy persists as a unique forum in which the voices, discussions and debates of all those interested in psychoanalytic ideas and their applications to the public sector can be heard.

With any changeover of editors, there is also inevitably some movement in the composition of the Editorial Boards. After many years of dedicated work for the journal, Marilyn Lawrence, Golan Shahar, Julia Fabricius, Richard Rusbridger and Francesca Hume are stepping down from their respective positions on the Editorial Board and Editorial Advisory Panel, and I would like to thank each of them for their vital contributions in supporting and shaping the direction of the journal. I also want to thank Tom Pennybacker who will be finishing his term as an Assistant Editor at the end of this year. I know that Tom has been essential in supporting Alessandra over the past three years and that he has also been invaluable in facilitating me in the first months of my new role.

responseSet.title: ‘Characters’ in psychoanalytic and interpersonal therapy: A comparison
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Characters’ in psychoanalytic and interpersonal therapy: A comparison

Laurence Spurling

Laurence Spurling

London, UK

Laurence Spurling  

characters, interpersonal therapy, psychoanalytic therapy, realms of reality

In this paper the author considers how the therapist might listen to the characters talked about by his or her patients. In psychoanalytic therapy the emphasis is on listening to the patient's characters as though they are located in psychic reality and as representatives of the transference relationship, whereas in interpersonal therapy (IPT) the patient's characters are taken as inhabiting the realm of external reality. It is argued that clinical thinking in IPT would be enhanced by taking more account of psychic reality, which will make clearer the quality of external reality in which the patient's characters are located. It is also argued that both therapies share an interest in enabling the patient to find characters which can serve as holograms of previously unexpressed affective experience.

responseSet.authorCitation: Carrington, A., Rock, B. & Stern, J.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic thinking in primary care: The Tavistock Psychotherapy Consultation model

Anca Carrington , Brian Rock and Julian Stern

Julian Stern

London, UK

Harrow, UK

Julian Stern  

applied psychoanalytic psychotherapy, medically unexplained symptoms (MUS), primary care, consultation, general practitioner (GP)

This paper describes the conceptual underpinnings, structure and operations of a novel service, the City and Hackney Primary Care Psychotherapy Consultation Service – a service set up partly with the aim of addressing the needs of patients who present with ‘medically unexplained symptoms’. As part of the Tavistock and Portman NHS Trust, this service moves its clinical base, staff members and daily work, as well as the foundations of psychoanalytic thinking that define the Trust's work, into the heart of a community, and provides psychoanalytically informed clinical practice and consultation to patients and general practitioners in the City and Hackney, one of London's (and the UK's) most deprived and ethnically diverse boroughs. The authors describe the psychoanalytical underpinnings of the model, the design and structure of the service, patient demographics and preliminary outcome data, as well as an example of consultation work with general practitioners. The authors propose that psychoanalytic applications have a place in primary care and that psychoanalytic thinking can help general practitioners and patients alike, even when the clinical interventions offered are not solely based on psychoanalytic technique or therapeutic approaches. The paper concludes with thoughts about the model, its origins and its future.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Male Homosexuality: Twentieth-Anniversary Edition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Male Homosexuality: Twentieth-Anniversary Edition

Poul Rohleder

Poul Rohleder


Poul Rohleder  

Kenneth Lewes, Plymouth, Jason Aronson, 2009, 334 pp., £31.95 (hardback), ISBN: 9780765706478

Although first published in 1988, this book seems an important work in relation to current psychoanalytic discussions, given the recent statement on homosexuality published by the British Psychoanalytic Council (2011). This 20th anniversary edition contains the text in its original form, with a foreword from Gilbert Cole and Donald Moss, and a new introduction by the author, reflecting on the 20 years since the release of the book.

Lewes provides a critical review of all the published psychoanalytic theories on homosexuality from Freud up until 1982, approximately 500 publications. A strength of his review is that it is informed by an ‘analytical’ analysis of the literature. It is a review written from within the psychoanalytic profession, using the discourse of psychoanalysis, rather than a critique from outside the discipline.

The book is written in 11 chapters, outlining distinct phases in the psychoanalytic writing on homosexuality (mostly male homosexuality). The review begins with the ideas of Freud. Freud considered human sexuality (including homosexuality) to develop from a constitutional bisexuality. Lewes argues that there is some ambiguity in Freud's understanding of the relationship between homosexuality and pathology, but that he did not seem to understand homosexuality as necessarily pathological. Freud understood homosexuality to be a result of an arrest in ‘normal’ sexual development; a variation of sexual development, not a deviation. Although an arrest of ‘normal’ sexual development can be thought of as pathological, Lewes points out how Freud distinguished between perversions (which for him were pathological) and inversion (referring to homosexuality), and wrote that inversion can be found in individuals who did not present with any significant deviation from the ‘normal’ (Freud, 1905). And in a famous letter to a mother of a homosexual son, Freud (1935) wrote that homosexuality ‘cannot be classified as an illness; we consider it to be a variation of the sexual function produced by a certain arrest of sexual development’. Freud (1921) also argued that homosexuals should be admitted into psychoanalytic training. From this beginning, Lewes goes on to document and give a thorough, detailed review of all psychoanalytic publications on homosexuality post-Freud, which on the whole departed from Freud's neutral, inquiring and more humane stance. Lewes shows how there emerged numerous theoretical accounts of homosexuality, which viewed homosexuality as a pathology, as a perversion, or form of character disturbance.

responseSet.title: The empathizing–systemizing (E–S) model of autism and psychoanalytic theories of truth, play and symbolization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The empathizing–systemizing (E–S) model of autism and psychoanalytic theories of truth, play and symbolization

Christopher Merritt

Christopher Merritt

London, UK

London, SE5 8AZ
UK

Christopher Merritt  

autism, autistic traits, empathizing–systemizing, psychic truth, psychoanalytic research

Little psychoanalytic material or theory features today in psychological work on autism and autistic traits and vice versa. Baron-Cohen's (2003) empathizing–systemizing (E–S) model of the autism spectrum posits two main clusters of autistic features: those surrounding impaired empathy, and those surrounding heightened systemizing behaviours. (Baron-Cohen, 2009) speculates that the common root of these two clusters is the individual's concept of truth. Psychoanalysis has addressed the related issue of internal and external reality since (Freud, 1897) differentiated ‘psychic’ from ‘historical’ truths. This could be of interest to those espousing the E–S model from a cognitive paradigm, as a ‘truth’-oriented cognitive style in autism may develop interdependently with the individual's experience of internal and external realities. Psychoanalysis has developed further ideas of relevance to the E–S model surrounding play and symbolization, the latter being an often-overlooked aspect of Wing and Gould's (1979) triad of autistic features. Seen from a psychodynamic perspective, the E–S model might provide a means to increase therapists' awareness of milder autistic traits amongst service users and analysands. Equally, it could offer a tool to structure empirical research into psychodynamic therapy with autism spectrum conditions.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

David Bell

David Bell

David Bell  

Dear Editor,

I was most unfortunately unable to attend the BPC conference ‘Psychoanalysis and homosexuality’, but thought it might be appropriate for me to inform your readership of our institute's position on this issue, especially as it is something that I have prioritised since taking up office as President.

I wish to make it clear that our policy as regards admission for training and progression in our institute is non-discriminatory as regards gay and lesbian people and we have been very actively involved in developing the BPCs position statement on this issue (which will soon be available). However, that being said I recognise it is one thing to have a policy and quite another to ensure that it is followed, not only to the letter but also in spirit. For we are all aware that prejudice can manifest itself in various forms and that it is fed by factors, both at the individual and at the institutional level, that can evade consciousness. We wish to ensure that gay and lesbian individuals feel free to apply to train as psychoanalysts at our Institute and that those accepted can progress through their training and develop professional careers free from prejudice, and this is ongoing work for us.

There is clearly a long way to go in terms of theoretical development and this is something that will involve the psychoanalytic community in general, whose contributions we will warmly welcome.

98

responseSet.title:
Dissatisfied psychotherapy patients: A tentative conceptual model
	 grounded in the participants' view
responseSet.authorMast: Camilla von Below and  Andrzej Werbart
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dissatisfied psychotherapy patients: A tentative conceptual model grounded in the participants' view

Camilla von Below and Andrzej Werbart

Andrzej Werbart

Tierp, Sweden

Stockholm, Sweden

Stockholm, Sweden

Andrzej Werbart  

psychoanalytic psychotherapy, process research, grounded theory, dissatisfied patients, young adults

Usually, between 5% and 20% of psychotherapy patients are dissatisfied with their treatments. This naturalistic study explores seven clearly dissatisfied patients' view of the therapeutic process and outcome. Interviews at termination of psychoanalytic psychotherapy and at a 1.5-year follow-up were analysed to create a tentative conceptual model of patient dissatisfaction using grounded theory approach. At the core of the model is an experience of abandonment by a therapist felt to be insufficiently flexible, a therapy lacking intensity, and links missing between therapy and everyday life. Dissatisfied patients lacked confidence in their relationship with the therapist, described their therapists in negative terms and concluded that their therapies lacked direction. They wanted more response from the therapist. Paying greater attention to the patient's emerging dissatisfaction may prevent lasting disappointment, unnecessary continuation of fruitless treatment, and probably increase efficiency.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma and Tom Pennybacker

Tom Pennybacker

Tom Pennybacker  

For the first issue of 2012 we are pleased to bring you a general issue: special issues allow us to deepen our thinking about a particular area, but general issues serve to remind us of the importance and variety of applications of analytic ideas in public health settings.

Psychoanalysis has focused a great deal on the importance of the relationship to the ‘breast’ during development. Jeremy Holmes opens this issue of the journal with an engaging paper focusing on the importance of the face as a ‘vehicle for generating meaning and representation’ (this issue) and explores the implications of this for psychotherapy.

One of the facts of life in psychotherapy is that when clinical material is presented, there will be as many opinions as there are psychotherapists in the room. Stephen Briggs and colleagues apply this fact of psychotherapy in its most creative form to generate an exchange of ideas about suicide as they discuss a clinical case from different perspectives, focusing in particular on the traumatising impact of suicide on the mind.

The papers by Cairns, Lunn & Poulsen and Keinänen and colleagues address very different subjects: the unconscious processes involved in the decision to place a family member with dementia into residential care, the manualisation of a psychoanalytic intervention for bulimic patients and a review of the literature on psychosocial risk factors for borderline personality disorder. These papers are united, however, by a willingness to study the question they address in a systematic fashion, with the help of research findings and/or methodology (qualitative and quantitative), thus highlighting the benefits of an ongoing relationship between psychoanalysis and research as one of several perspectives that can add to our practice. In our current times, with our organisations facing – and being overwhelmed by – external pressures and the evidence-based practice agenda, this dialogue is more important than ever.

1

responseSet.title: Emotional processing and chronic fatigue syndrome
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emotional processing and chronic fatigue syndrome

Trudie Chalder and Kirsty Hill

Kirsty Hill

London, UK

Guy's Campus
London, UK

Kirsty Hill  

chronic fatigue syndrome, treatment, emotions

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS) is characterised by severe fatigue and is associated with functional impairment. Somatic symptoms are often present. Although much debate exists about the aetiology of CFS, misinterpretations in the cognitive processing of emotions has been suggested as a causal factor in vulnerable individuals. The rehabilitative treatments of cognitive behavioural therapy and graded exercise therapy have been shown to be effective in improving fatigue and disability in CFS. To date these are the only evidence-based treatments, but effect sizes are modest. Some patients may benefit from receiving more emotion-focused treatments once their functioning has improved. The aim of this paper is to explore the literature on emotional expression and processing in the context of CFS.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma  

After three years as Editor of this journal, this is my final editorial. It has been a privilege to work on this journal and I am very grateful to the APP for giving me this opportunity. As an Editor one is engaged in an enriching cycle of CPD (Continuing Professional Development): every paper is a window into the inspiring work being carried out by colleagues in the UK and abroad. Their work sustains a psychoanalytic presence in the public healthcare and voluntary services and in academic research settings. This presence has always been important, but it has become all the more important to raise its profile through publications in our current context – one in which psychoanalysis is being marginalized and in which several psychoanalytic services in the UK are under threat and a few have been closed down.

The publication of clinical work remains very close to the heart of this Journal, but in the current climate research has become vital to our sustainability in the long term, and hence over the past three years the Journal has increased the publication of research papers. Dialogue with other disciplines and therapeutic modalities is also important to the evolution of our discipline, and hence we have also prioritised these publications.

In this issue, Hobson, Cooper and Wren ably illustrate the importance of an open yet critical engagement with the relationship between ‘scientific evidence’ and practice. Werbart et al. remind us of how research can inform practice by studying patients who are dissatisfied with their experiences of psychoanalytic psychotherapy having completed the planned course of treatment. Spurling looks at what psychoanalytic psychotherapy can learn from another modality – interpersonal psychotherapy – as well as what it has to offer to this modality through exploring the delicate balance between emphasising external and psychic reality when hearing and responding to a patient's material. Finally, Levander and Holmqvist share some research based on responses on the Rorschach as a prognostic indication of long-term recovery from psychosis.

responseSet.title: Is it time for Hippocrates and Freud to have a joint consultation? MUS patients – A reflection from the front line
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is it time for Hippocrates and Freud to have a joint consultation? MUS patients – A reflection from the front line

Richard Taylor

Richard Taylor

Aberdeen, UK

Richard Taylor  

medically unexplained symptoms, doctor–patient relationship, countertransference enactments, equafinality

Patients with ‘medically unexplained symptoms’ (MUS) remain an ongoing challenge to medical practitioners using conventional approaches to diagnosis and treatment. This challenge often creates tensions and defensive enactments in the doctor–patient relationship. In this viewpoint article, a personal reflection from current clinical practice is presented as a catalyst for further dialogue. In particular, psychoanalytic thinking around the issue of MUS is advocated as bringing a valuable focus by looking at ‘meanings’ behind symptoms. Medical practitioners may benefit considerably by working jointly with psychoanalytical practitioners. This could improve the care of patients with MUS and in addition bring beneficial understanding to the dynamics of the patient relationship.

Rorschach human content responses as a prognostic indication of long-term recovery from psychosis

Sonja Levander and Ragnhild Holmqvist

Ragnhild Holmqvist

Stockholm, Sweden

Stockholm, Sweden

Ragnhild Holmqvist  

Rorschach, psychosis, personality, recovery, prediction

Rorschach pre-treatment protocols from 32 first-time psychotic patients were correlated to outcome levels five years later (GAF). Human content responses (HR) were found to be positively related to future functioning level, when they appear in emotionally loaded contexts. Possible ways of understanding these results are discussed from a psychodynamic perspective.

responseSet.title: Therapeutic residential care for children and young people: An attachment and trauma-informed model for practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Therapeutic residential care for children and young people: An attachment and trauma-informed model for practice

Stuart Hannah

Stuart Hannah


www.nscap.org.uk

Stuart Hannah  

Susan Barton, Rudy Gonzalez and Patrick Tomlinson, London, Jessica Kingsley Publishers, 2011, 288 pp., £22.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-1849052559

This truly global book is the product of an interesting and creative collaboration between leaders and practitioners in residential group treatment settings in Australia (The Lighthouse Community) and the UK (The Cotswold Community, SACCS). It is a long overdue contribution towards the theory base required to equip people working in multiple roles in environments that aim to be healing in its broadest sense. I enjoyed it as much for the elements of human testimony as for the attempts made to link eclectic theory with practice.

Mindful that this journal is entitled Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, it is possibly worth reinforcing the eclectic nature of the theoretical territory covered in this book. Chapter 1 ranges from individual, family, community and society wellness theory, psychodynamic thinking and concepts to attachment and trauma-informed theories. The journey is a rich and varied one and no doubt reflects the complexity inherent in this type of work in these types of settings. Those of you working with children will enjoy details like the front cover picture of a multi-coloured child's ‘home’ with love heart-shaped windows, and the opening quote in the introduction of an ancient Chinese proverb, ‘A child's life is like a piece of paper, on which every passer-by leaves a mark’.

As someone already converted to the cause of group care and treatment homes needing to be greenhouses not warehouses, my only personal struggle with the style of this book is the relentless use of subheadings that appear literally on every other page. Whilst this gives the book a very accessible, dip in-able quality, it also obstructs the ‘uninterrupted feed’ experience that can accompany the reading of a good old-fashioned solid chapter. I assume that the authors have intended that the book appear more accessible and less daunting to the more pragmatic and less academically minded amongst the sector and potential readership.

responseSet.title: The master and his emissary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The master and his emissary

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

UK

Jeremy Holmes  

Iain McGilchrist, New Haven, CT, Yale University Press, 2009, 534 pp., £11.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-0300168921

How times change. In the 1950s Charles Brenner's Elementary textbook of psychoanalysis sold over a million copies, unthinkable today. In my more cynical moments I sometimes wonder whether in our field now writers outnumber readers – everyone is writing books, and no one is reading them. However, every decade or so, a volume appears that makes the mysterious leap from the professional ghetto into the wider cultural field. An example for your reviewer's generation was R.D. Laing's 1960s bestseller The divided self. Almost exactly 50 years later, The master and his emissary – whose first half's subtitle, ‘The divided brain’, echoes Laing – is destined to be another. I urge all recalcitrant readers to peep out from their professional silos and get a copy. It contains really important messages for psychotherapists.

The author is a man of exceptional gifts: Winchester and Oxford scholar, congratulatory First, thrice Fellow of All Soul's, fluent in classical Greek and Latin, French, German and (possibly) Russian. Like many outstanding doctors, he came to medicine ‘late’, after a distinguished career in literary studies, perhaps drawn to psychiatry as the most humanistic (albeit also the most capable of degradation) of the medical specialties.

The master and his emissary

Exploring the implications of the asymmetry between the brain's Right and Left hemispheres, the book is an extravaganza of dichotomising. Like its subject, it falls into two distinct halves. The first is an account of the neurobiology and psychology of the cerebral hemispheres, based mainly on the classical ‘split brain’ studies of Sperry and others, both on experimental animals and on human subjects. It also includes findings from the contrasting neuropsychology of stroke victims, depending on which hemisphere is affected.

Based on this, McGilchrist constructs two contrasting cerebral ‘personalities’, or ways of being-in-the-world (a phrase with Heideggerian overtones). The world ‘according to’ the Right hemisphere is individualised, personal, uncertain, embodied, preverbal and experiential. It arises out of ‘betweenness’, the encounter with the Other, whether in the natural or human world. The Right's skill lies in attending to and connecting with this outside world; it is highly sensitive to context, so that meanings are not abstract and divorced from reality, but situational and provisional.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Bernard Ratigan

Bernard Ratigan


Leicester,

Bernard Ratigan  

Dear Editor,

About 250 attended the overdue conference in January 2012 on ‘Psychoanalysis and homosexuality: Moving on’. It was more a milestone on a long journey than a destination. It would have been helpful to know the demographics of the attendees in terms of professional affiliation, location, ethnicity, sexual orientation and gender. My impression was that many of the attendees were not members of the BPC (British Psychonalytic Council) and were not psychoanalysts. If this is a correct assumption, it is sad, and needs thinking about in terms of the ‘What next?’ question.

I am in a rather anomalous position being the recipient of a NOW, a BCP sponsored award for outstanding professional leadership but not a member of the BPC, although clinically finding myself much more in sympathy with it than some colleagues in the UKCP (United Kingdom Council for Psychotherapy). There was a strong sense of déjà vu: it was as if the last 30+ years, since it was made clear to me that training as a psychoanalyst was not for gay people, had never occurred. I had gone to make my career in provincial settings where being out as a psychoanalytic psychotherapist who was gay never seemed a big deal, and the influence this has enabled me to have on clinical practice with LGBT (Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender) patients and in specialist psychoanalytic psychotherapy training has been at the core of my work.

Hearing the history of the psychoanalytic engagement with homosexuality and the consequent perversion, domestication and gradual growing heteronormativity of psychoanalysis as the twentieth century wore on was, of course, as it always is, useful. My problem was that I have been banging on about it for so long: I had to remind myself that for some present it might have been news. It was hard to believe that recent products of training in psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy had not been through all of this. If not, why not, I asked myself? If training is integrating diversity into the curricula, why was the conference necessary? What have the trainings that receive any form of public money been doing all these years? There is a serious question of accountability here that needs thinking about.

responseSet.title: Response to: Research off the couch: re-visiting the transsexual conundrum
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to: Research off the couch: re-visiting the transsexual conundrum

Nicola Barden

Nicola Barden

UK

Nicola Barden  

Firstly, I want to thank Alessandra for such a thoughtful and thought-provoking paper on a subject that is challenging on many levels.

She has carefully and rightly emphasised the plural aetiologies of transsexuality, which is not reducible to medical or socio-cultural origins. This of course leaves room for psychoanalytic understanding and Alessandra gives a compelling description of the importance of the returned gaze in identity formation; the recognition of other then brings the other into a state of self-recognition. One of the things I am doing in my reading of this paper, and I think this is what the paper means us to do, is to avoid conflation of description with definition. Before we think of the transsexual dilemma we need to think of our own position, the position of psychoanalysis, in relation to this dilemma. We need to think of the ground on which we stand.

Alessandra has drawn out the challenges that still exist in this country for those who openly theorise from ‘not heterosexuality’. Now transsexuality is of course not directly about ‘not heterosexuality’. As Spry (1997) wrote in her memoir, ‘Orlando's Sleep’, ‘It was my gender that was crossed, not my sexuality,’ as she transitioned from being a straight man to a lesbian woman: her object of desire remained stable. One cannot assume that transsexuality carries homosexuality with it. Yet, the point about how psychoanalysis has carried them both is well made, and is something that I will return to. For the moment, my point is that the question of causation has been much debated for both, but inevitably within a context of problem and pathology. While this remains the case, questions about aetiology inevitably carry agendas, all too often crudely so: what went wrong and can analysis fix it? The problem is individualised. As long as the individual bears the weight of this, it is impossible to expect honesty or transparency, and analysis has little chance of approaching a ‘truth’. As long as analysis starts from positions of deviance or variance, it is impossible to hope for answers to questions of aetiology.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic psychotherapy for bulimia nervosa: A manualized approach
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy for bulimia nervosa: A manualized approach

Susanne Lunn and Stig Poulsen

Stig Poulsen

Denmark
Department of Psychology

Stig Poulsen  

bulimia nervosa, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychotherapy manual, psychopathology, eating disorders

A condensed version of a manual for two-year psychoanalytic psychotherapy for bulimia nervosa, developed for a clinical trial comparing psychoanalytic psychotherapy and cognitive behaviour therapy, is presented. The conceptualization of the psychopathology is outlined followed by a presentation of the specific principles of psychoanalytic psychotherapy for bulimia nervosa. The phases of the therapy and the adaptation of the therapy to different patterns of personality are described, and principles for training and supervision are outlined.

responseSet.title: Childhood depression: a place for psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Childhood depression: a place for psychotherapy

Graham Music

Graham Music

London, UK

Graham Music  

Judith Trowell, with Gillian Miles, London, Karnac, 2011, 320 pp., RRP £23.74

This book is based on a study that has been extremely influential in getting psychoanalytic psychotherapy back on the map in the public sector. It is important for many reasons, not least that it was the first outcome study using psychoanalytic child psychotherapy that is cited in a National Institute for Clinical Evidence (NICE) guideline, the one for childhood depression. This is a major achievement and has meant that it could be argued at national and local levels that psychoanalytic child psychotherapy must be available to the National Health Service (NHS) and local Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) services due to being prescribed by NICE as an effective evidence-based treatment. This study demonstrated the efficacy of non-cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) psychotherapeutic treatments for childhood depression at a point when in many child mental health services the choice, at best, is between medication and CBT. It is also an example of research which set out to be non-partisan and aimed to investigate two central therapeutic modalities, psychoanalytic child psychotherapy and systemic family therapy, in a spirit of open-mindedness and lack of rivalry. These have been the two primary modalities central to therapeutic work in the Tavistock's Child and Family Department for many years, modalities that have long rubbed shoulders but not always without friction.

The vision for such a study could only have come from a clinician who is able to cross boundaries and therapeutic borders with both ease and dignity, a practicing and experienced psychoanalytic clinician who is theoretically sophisticated but whose primary interest is in thinking about what is best for patients and who has little time to waste on tribal rivalries. Dr Judith Trowell, as many know, is just such a person, a child psychiatrist and psychoanalyst with a distinguished record of achievements who has put her head above the parapet many times in the spirit of innovation, and this book is another example of this. Her other achievements include her expertise in working with judges and the legal system, a Professor who started an innovative child mental health training, being Chair of the Child and Family Department at the Tavistock for many years, as well as setting up the Monroe Young Family Service which became a beacon of good practice in providing both interventions and assessments for the most worrying families, those in which removal of children by social services was very likely.

responseSet.title: In sickness and in health: An exploration of some of the unconscious processes involved in the decision by family caregivers to place a family member with dementia in residential care
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In sickness and in health: An exploration of some of the unconscious processes involved in the decision by family caregivers to place a family member with dementia in residential care

Margaret Cairns

Margaret Cairns

UK

Margaret Cairns  

dementia, carers, evacuatory projection, disenfranchized grief, maternal ambivalence

This paper explores the feelings, conscious and unconscious, evoked by the difficult decision to place a family member with dementia into residential care. In an exploratory study I interviewed five people who had made this decision with a view to exploring some of the unconscious processes involved in their decision-making. The interviews were interpreted paying attention to my understanding of the transference and my countertransference feelings and as triangulated by a psychodynamic supervision group.

Intimate contact with ageing and death evokes fears about mortality and activates primitive anxieties. The progress of dementia may amplify primitive or psychotic states of functioning, particularly those involving evacuatory projection, and family carers may resort to splitting off unbearable feelings of anxiety which such projections can evoke and may contribute to their decision to look for residential care.

Once placed in residential care different dynamics evolve and further difficult questions arise: how often to visit; whether and when to dispose of the person's possessions and ultimately how to grieve. The focus of this paper is, as far as it is possible, on the partner without dementia as an individual and the impact of their relative's dementia on their lives.

[[RunningHead]]

The onset of a chronic disease as a traumatic psychic experience: A psychodynamic survey on type 1 diabetes in young patients

Franco D'Alberton , Laura Nardi and Stefano Zucchini

Stefano Zucchini

Bologna, Italy

Stefano Zucchini  

diabetes, psychotherapy, chronic illness, child

Type 1 insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM 1) is a chronic disease with a sudden onset often during childhood and adolescence. The onset of diabetes implies a radical change in personal and family habits. The right insulin therapy accompanied by a correct diet and physical activity is required in order to reduce hypoglycaemic and hyperglycaemic episodes, and the outcome of long-term diabetic complications is first and foremost a medical problem. It is well known, however, that despite many medical and technical developments, metabolic control in children and adolescents with IDDM 1 has not improved as much as it had been expected. It is only in recent years that clinicians have focused on other factors that may be involved in the process of treating diabetes. The sudden onset of this chronic illness often has the features of a traumatic emotional experience that exceeds the subject's resilience and capacity to deal with it and elaborate it in psychic terms. This paper explores some of the anxieties that may influence a good adherence to the medical therapy, taking into account the possible influence of the psychic trauma that may frequently be associated with the onset of a chronic illness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Suicide and trauma: A case discussion

Stephen Briggs , Mark Goldblatt , Reinhard Lindner , John Maltsberger and Georg Fiedler

Georg Fiedler

Hamburg
Germany

USA

London
UK

Georg Fiedler  

suicide, trauma, mourning, identifications, affect-regulation, symbolic thinking

In this paper we explore the relationship between trauma and suicidal thoughts and behaviour. This exploration has a double perspective: a deeper understanding of trauma can illuminate the dynamics of suicidality; and the psychotherapeutic study of suicidal people increases our understanding of the impact of trauma. This discussion addresses three particular configurations: (a) the impact on the individual of the suicide of a close person; (b) the traumatic dimension of suicidal behaviour on the self; and (c) the impact of childhood traumatic experiences on the subsequent development of suicide. We describe the psychotherapy of a suicidal man, who had suffered many losses including that of his mother to suicide, as the basis for commentary by three discussants. The paper concludes that the approach linking trauma with suicide is a fruitful one and further study is recommended.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research off the couch: Re-visiting the transsexual conundrum

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK


London, UK

Alessandra Lemma  

attachment, body modification, body image, mentalizing, mirroring, transsexuality

This paper outlines the findings of a qualitative study involving interviews with eight transsexual individuals who had volunteered to take part in a television (TV) documentary about transsexuality. The participants were interviewed before taking part in the documentary and after its screening on national TV. The author proposes that for either biological and/or psychological reasons, some transsexual individuals experience early on a profound and disturbing incongruence at the level of the bodily self. In turn, this experience of incongruity is not contingently mirrored by primary attachment figures and remains unmentalized, thus disrupting self-coherence. In an attempt to restore self-coherence, the individual searches for the ‘right’ body that is anticipated to relieve the felt incongruity. The way this modified body relieves the incongruity is through the certainty it imparts that the image in the ‘mirror’ will match the subjective experience of the body. In this study, the experience of being mirrored through the group process during filming and the audience's response to the TV series, which was very positive, appeared to be associated with an attenuation in the urgency with which planned body modifications were being discussed post-TV screenings.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A mentalization-based approach to the understanding and treatment of functional somatic disorders

Patrick Luyten , Boudewijn van Houdenhove , Alessandra Lemma , Mary Target and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Leuven, Belgium

London, UK

London, UK

Peter Fonagy  

functional somatic disorders, somatoform disorders, mentalization, attachment, stress

Patients with functional somatic disorders (FSD) represent a sizeable group in our health care system. FSD are associated with high health care use and considerable personal and economic costs. Evidence-based treatments for FSD are only modestly effective in a large subgroup of patients, particularly in the long run, which emphasizes the need to develop more effective treatments rooted in extant knowledge about the nature of FSD. This paper presents a contemporary psychodynamic perspective on the conceptualization and treatment of patients with FSD rooted in attachment and mentalization theory. First, we review animal and human research demonstrating the close relationships among attachment, stress regulation, and immune and pain-regulating systems. We highlight research findings concerning the high interpersonal and metabolic costs associated with the use of insecure secondary attachment strategies (i.e. attachment deactivating and hyperactivating strategies) leading to increased vulnerability for stress. Next, we review evidence for the role of impairments in (embodied) mentalization in patients with FSD both as cause and consequence of functional somatic complaints, leading to the re-emergence of so-called non-mentalizing modes, i.e. modes of subjectivity that antedate the capacity for full mentalizing. Based on these views, a novel brief psychodynamic intervention for patients with functional somatic complaints is presented.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The manual of panic focused psychodynamic psychotherapy – Extended range

Arabella Kurtz

Arabella Kurtz


Arabella Kurtz  

F.N. Busch, B.L. Milrod, M.B. Singer and A.C. Aronson, Routledge Mental Health, Abingdon, Routledge, 2011, 230 pp., £21.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-0415871600

The manual of panic focused psychodynamic psychotherapy – Extended range (PFPP-XR) is part of a welcome move to develop a systematic approach to the provision of psychodynamic psychotherapies in order to build scientific support for their use. It is the sequel to a manual published in 1997, which presented PFPP as a form of brief psychodynamic psychotherapy for patients presenting with panic or anxiety in order that research into its outcome could be carried out. Lest you find the less-than-snappy title of this manual off-putting, I will say at the outset what an interesting and informative read it is. It provides a clear and concise description of key psychodynamic concepts before launching into an instructive and thoroughly demystifying account of a form of brief psychotherapy, PFPP, amply illustrated with case examples. It is especially valuable as a teaching tool because it gives such a good working sense of a particular form of psychodynamic therapy.

PFPP is described as a brief 24-session form of psychotherapy for patients suffering from panic and anxiety with a focus on the relief of symptoms through gaining an understanding of the role of unconscious motivation and affect in their production. The revised manual clarifies and extends the original PFPP model on the back of 10 years' clinical experience and research, with a new emphasis in the third part of the book on how it can be applied to a wide range of anxiety problems that fit diagnostic criteria in DSM-IV. Evolution of the therapeutic approach is presented as the result of the authors' considerable experience of using it; what is not perhaps as clear as it should be is the way in which research has informed clinical practice over the past decade. Indeed, research into the effectiveness of PFPP is referred to if and when relevant, rather than laid out for a fuller discussion. For the research-minded, who in the terms of the manual ought to be increasing in number, it would have been well worthwhile including a chapter on the implications of research for practice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Broken Bounds: Contemporary reflections on the antisocial tendency

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley


Jessica Yakeley  

Christopher Reeves, Karnac Books, 2012, 160 pp. (paperback)

This slim and readable volume dedicated to exploring Donald Winnicott's concept of the ‘antisocial tendency’ is to be welcomed for its contemporary relevance and appeal to all those working with psychologically disturbed youngsters. The book is the publication of a series of six child and adolescent lectures on the theme of the antisocial tendency, organized by the Squiggle Foundation in 2009/2010 and given by child and adolescent psychotherapists and other leading professionals who have extensive experience of working in the fields of child mental health, residential child care and child care policy. Because each speaker was given little specific brief in relation to the theme of the antisocial tendency and was unaware of the content of the other lectures in the series, the publication of their talks has resulted in some overlap and repetition regarding content and context. However, this is outweighed by the immediacy and evocativeness of the, often very personal, accounts of each speaker's interpretation and experience of Winnicott's thinking on antisocial youth, including some vivid case examples (see the chapters by Ann Horne and Jenny Sprince), accounts that are accessible to a non-specialist audience and readership but are deeply moving.

These papers confirm Winnicott's continued significance in the twenty-first century. In the UK, the unforeseen 2011 summer riots lend added poignancy to the publication of these lectures. The public disorder which so suddenly and dramatically erupted is explored in a postscript by Adrian Ward to the original lectures. He suggests that the rioting represents a form of communication which reflects the current discontent and despair of a very deprived and disadvantaged section of our country's youth, comprising young people who know no other way of communicating their distress except through action. The behaviour expresses resentment, but also hope and the wish to be stopped. I am sure Winnicott would be in full agreement with this analysis.

responseSet.title: A brief introduction to psychoanalytic theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A brief introduction to psychoanalytic theory

Helen Barker

Helen Barker

London, UK

Helen Barker  

Stephen Frosh, Palgrave Macmillan, 2012, 262 pp., £14.99 (paperback)

What books can we recommend to someone starting to learn about psychoanalysis and psychotherapy? There are those books which focus on psychoanalysis such as Bateman and Holmes (1995) or Budd and Rusbridger (2005) or, more recently, Milton, Polmear and Fabricius (2010). For a broader approach to psychotherapy, there is no better place to start than the classic text, now in its fourth edition, from Bateman, Brown and Pedder (2010). Or we could suggest a more general introduction to psychoanalytic thinking such as the Talking Cure from the Tavistock Clinic Series (Taylor, 1999) or, my personal favourite, the pithy and wise book by Salzberger-Wittenberg (1973). What these books share in common is that they are written by highly experienced clinicians who are committed to psychoanalytic concepts and to explaining the value of these ideas to a new audience. Even when they acknowledge and address the critiques of psychoanalysis, as Milton et al. do in a thoughtful and respectful way, we know that they are doing so from a particular perspective.

Stephen Frosh, Professor of Psychology in the Department of Psychosocial Studies at Birkbeck College, has written an intelligent, informed and engaging book from a third position. He is neither for nor against psychoanalysis but he offers us a vivid and comprehensible account of psychoanalytic theory and its relevance to contemporary thought. He is not writing from a clinical perspective, nor from the standpoint of psychoanalytic devotee. Although he describes himself as writing from ‘a largely sympathetic point of view’, on the first page he states ‘the standing of psychoanalysis as a discipline possessing valid knowledge about human psychology remains uncertain’. Later in the same chapter in a section entitled ‘Precarious psychoanalysis’, he discusses the uncertainty about the future of psychoanalysis and its ‘important weaknesses’ as a discipline. However, at its best, says Frosh, psychoanalysis is something ‘curious and provocative’, ‘serious, slow, thoughtful, uncertain and complicated’. In his conclusion, he states that ‘psychoanalysis asks painful questions of reality and insists that we do not back away from the answers’, a necessary endeavour ‘if life is to be lived ethically and to the full’. His stance seems to be an exemplary analytic one, neither idealising nor attacking his subject. He demonstrates a healthy ambivalence which creates a space in which new understandings emerge.

[[RunningHead]]

Seeing, sitting and lying down: Reflections on the role of visual communication in analytic therapy

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

Barnstaple
UK

Jeremy Holmes  

symbolization, the face, attachment, mirroring

The paper develops the post-Winnicottian psychoanalyst Kenneth Wright's theory of the face which he sees as insufficiently theorized psychoanalytically. As compared with the breast, the face is intrinsically a vehicle for generating meaning and representation, rather than satisfying bodily need. Infants see themselves reflected back in the mother's facial expressions and this helps to build up self-representation in the child's mind's eye. Other Attachment-influenced research findings about care-giver/infant facial communication are also described: partial contingency, triangulation and rupture/repair. The relevance of this to the question of chair or couch-based therapy is discussed. The discussion is illustrated by a consideration of the work of the visual artist Louise Bourgeois.

responseSet.title: Response to Alessandra Lemma – APP Lecture Research off the couch: revisiting the transsexual conundrum
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Alessandra Lemma – APP Lecture Research off the couch: revisiting the transsexual conundrum

Domenico Di Ceglie

Domenico Di Ceglie


Tavistock Centre
London, UK

London, UK

Domenico Di Ceglie  

These comments are based on a developmental perspective as my experience derives primarily from work with children, adolescents and their families attending a gender identity development service.

The paper stresses the importance of empirical research, in this case qualitative, as a complementary source of evidence to that derived from psychotherapeutic or psychoanalytic work. This stance is in my view valuable and needs to be well supported. The empirical observation in this paper contributes to a better understanding of the development of atypical gender identity which in some forms is addressed as transsexualism. The language to define these experiences is complex and controversial as it refers to a multitude of different experiences regarding one's own perceptions of gender identity in relationship to the sexual appearance of the body. In childhood, definitions of atypical gender development have gone from ‘gender identity disorder’ to the more acceptable definitions by families and professionals of Gender Dysphoria, Gender Diversity or Gender Variance. The paper stresses the importance of regarding transsexual experience as varied. A fitting metaphor for this would be the Ai Wey Wey Sun Flower Seeds Installation at the Tate Modern. This work is composed of about 120 million porcelain seeds individually painted by a number of workers. At a distance they all look the same, but at a closer look, in spite of the communality of some features, they all differ because of the individual difference of the paint strokes, as Ai Wey Wey himself emphasised in an introductory interview for the Tate exhibition.

From the experiences reported by the trans people involved in a television programme, Lemma put forward a hypothesis that a lack of experience of mirroring of their perceived gender identity by the attachment figure contributes to their need for acceptance, particularly in the visual field. This becomes an ongoing pursuit.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma and Brian Rock

Brian Rock

Brian Rock  

That the body is the starting point of mental functioning was core to Freud's (1923) own understanding of the development of the ego. As he famously put it, the ego is ‘first and foremost a body-ego; it is not merely a surface entity but it is itself the projection of a surface’ (Freud, 1923, p. 26), that is, the most primitive form of self-representation is first and foremost a body representation. Freud understood that psychic development is rooted in the body, and consequently emphasised the measure of the demand for work imposed on the psyche owing to its link with the body. Since then psychoanalytic practitioners have been committed to understanding the problematic relationship between the body and mind. Winnicott was especially attuned to the importance of the body for psychic development, observing that ‘The basis of a self forms on the fact of the body’ (Winnicott, 1970, p. 270). He was also mindful of the challenge presented by our embodied nature:

How easy it is to take for granted the lodgment of the psyche in the body and to forget that this again is an achievement. It is an achievement which by no means falls to the lot of all. (Winnicott, 1988, p. 122)

This ‘lodgment’ – Winnicott (1970) elsewhere referred to it through the somewhat poetic image of the ‘psyche in-dwelling in the soma’ – brings home the rootedness of mental structures in early sensory and affective experience (Fonagy & Target, 2007).

Psychoanalysis has a long, rich clinical tradition in the area of psychosomatic medicine through the examination of the struggle we all face representing our lived, embodied experience. This relies on the capacity to symbolise, which can be impaired or undeveloped for a number of reasons (Segal, 1957). Moreover, when our experience is of a traumatic, distressing kind, the capacity to represent it in a symbolic manner can be severely undermined. Here the body may become the canvas on which psychic pain is depicted. Understanding the possible and layered psychic meanings of these bodily phenomena for the individual is a central focus of the psychoanalytic therapeutic process (Bronstein, 2011).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A systematic review of the evidence-based psychosocial risk factors for understanding of borderline personality disorder

Matti Keinänen , Jeffrey Johnson , Elizabeth Richards and Elizabeth Courtney

Elizabeth Courtney

New York
USA

Turku
Finland

Kirkkotie

New York
USA

Elizabeth Courtney  

borderline personality disorder, psychosocial risk factors, evidence-based medicine, psychotherapy, aetiology

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a serious personality disorder characterized by affective instability, impulsivity and interpersonal disturbance. Currently, intensive research is being conducted concerning the aetiology of BPD, including research on neurobiological, temperamental, psychosocial and cultural risk factors. This study focuses on psychosocial risk factors while other risk factors are taken into account in the discussion of the development of BPD. To our knowledge, no systematic review of the evidence-based medicine literature concerning this theme has been made thus far. However, understanding psychosocial risk factors of BPD is important in order to develop psychotherapeutic treatment models and methods. We provide a systematic review of the literature focusing on psychosocial risk factors for BPD. Utilizing this knowledge, we discuss how these data may be used when studying the development of borderline personality disorder and the treatment of borderline personality disorder.
